session.user:
Read these source files and report back their key patterns:
1. `src/erk/tui/commands/types.py` - Find the `CommandDefinition` class, especially the `launch_key` field (line ~60) 2. `src/erk/tui/commands/registry.py` - Find: - All `launch_key` assignments in `get_all_commands()` (report which commands have which launch keys) - The `_key_to_command_id` dict construction - Line numbers for all launch key assignments (lines ~230-260)
Report exact line numbers, field definitions, and all launch key assignments.
FIRST check docs/learned/index.md for existing documentation on this topic. Read relevant docs before exploring raw files. Then explore codebase as necessary.

Add docs here for cross-cutting technical patterns. 12→- [capabilities/](capabilities/) 13→- [changelog/](changelog/) 14→- [checklists/](checklists/) 15→- [ci/](ci/) 16→- [claude-code/](claude-code/) 17→- [cli/](cli/) — Explore when building CLI commands or output formatting. session.assistant: Good! I found relevant documentation. Let me read the TUI-specific docs that are most relevant:
session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→title: TUI Command Registration 3→read_when: 4→ - "adding a new TUI command to the registry" 5→ - "understanding the 3-place coordination pattern for TUI commands" 6→ - "working with TUI command categories or display formatters" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "adding a new TUI command without updating all 3 places" 9→ warning: "TUI commands require 3-place coordination: registry definition, display formatter, and action inventory. See tui-command-registration.md." 10→ - action: "adding a CLI flag that affects behavior without checking TUI command palette" 11→ warning: "TUI command palette generates shell commands via src/erk/tui/commands/registry.py. When adding CLI flags that change behavior, check if TUI-generated commands need the flag too." 12→--- 13→ 14→# TUI Command Registration 15→ 16→Adding a TUI command requires coordinating three places in the codebase. 17→ 18→## 3-Place Coordination Pattern 19→ 20→When adding a command, update all three: 21→ 22→1. **Display formatter**: A `_display_*()` function that generates the display name 23→2. **CommandDefinition**: Entry in `get_all_commands()` with id, name, category, availability, and display getter 24→3. **Action inventory**: Command appears in the correct category 25→ 26→All definitions are in `src/erk/tui/commands/registry.py`. 27→ 28→## Example: `codespace_run_plan` 29→ 30→**Display formatter** (lines 135-137):
... [36 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: Command Execution Strategies 3→read_when: 4→ - "executing commands in TUI" 5→ - "choosing between sync and streaming execution" 6→ - "implementing command runners" 7→tripwires: 8→ - action: "using subprocess.Popen in TUI code without stdin=subprocess.DEVNULL" 9→ warning: "Child processes inherit stdin from parent; in TUI context this creates deadlocks when child prompts for user input. Always set `stdin=subprocess.DEVNULL` for TUI subprocess calls." 10→last_audited: "2026-02-16 08:00 PT" 11→audit_result: clean 12→--- 13→ 14→# Command Execution Strategies 15→ 16→This guide covers the different strategies for executing commands in erk and when to use each approach. 17→ 18→## Two Strategies 19→ 20→### 1. Streaming Subprocess (Preferred) 21→ 22→**Pattern:** Execute command in subprocess, stream output back to UI in real-time 23→ 24→**Characteristics:** 25→ 26→- Real-time output visibility 27→- Non-blocking UI 28→- Requires repository context (`repo_root`) 29→- Uses background thread for I/O 30→- Cross-thread communication with `app.call_from_thread()`
... [165 lines omitted] ...

1→--- 2→title: Textual CommandPalette Guide 3→read_when: 4→ - "implementing command palette in Textual TUI" 5→ - "hiding system commands from command palette" 6→ - "get_system_commands method" 7→ - "removing Keys Quit Screenshot Theme from palette" 8→ - "adding emoji prefixes to command palette entries" 9→ - "using CommandCategory for command categorization" 10→last_audited: "2026-02-05 00:00 PT" 11→audit_result: edited 12→--- 13→ 14→# Textual CommandPalette Guide 15→ 16→For standard Textual CommandPalette patterns (Providers, Hit/DiscoveryHit, Matcher, async generators, Screen.COMMANDS), consult the [Textual documentation](https://textual.textualize.io/). This doc focuses on erk-specific patterns and a critical Textual pitfall. 17→ 18→## System Commands Pitfall 19→ 20→**CRITICAL:** `get_system_commands` must be overridden on the **App class**, not the Screen class. Textual calls `app.get_system_commands(screen)` when opening the palette — it never calls this method on screens. 21→ 22→```python 23→# WRONG — This method is never called by Textual 24→class MyModalScreen(ModalScreen): 25→ def get_system_commands(self, screen: Screen) -> Iterator[SystemCommand]: 26→ return iter(()) # Has no effect! 27→ 28→# CORRECT — Override on App class 29→class MyApp(App): 30→ def get_system_commands(self, screen: Screen) -> Iterator[SystemCommand]:
... [39 lines omitted] ...
session.assistant: Perfect! Now I have the documentation context. Let me read the actual source files to get the specific line numbers and patterns:
session.tool_result:
1→"""Data types for command palette.""" 2→ 3→from collections.abc import Callable 4→from dataclasses import dataclass 5→from enum import Enum, auto 6→ 7→from erk.tui.data.types import PlanRowData 8→from erk.tui.views.types import ViewMode 9→ 10→ 11→class CommandCategory(Enum): 12→ """Category of a command in the command palette. 13→ 14→ Used to determine the emoji prefix displayed in the palette. 15→ """ 16→ 17→ ACTION = auto() # ⚡ Mutative operations 18→ OPEN = auto() # 🔗 Browser navigation 19→ COPY = auto() # 📋 Clipboard operations 20→ 21→ 22→@dataclass(frozen=True) 23→class CommandContext: 24→ """Context available to commands. 25→ 26→ Attributes: 27→ row: The plan row data for the selected plan 28→ view_mode: The active view mode (plans, learn, objectives) 29→ """ 30→
... [38 lines omitted] ...

1→"""Command registry for command palette. 2→ 3→This module defines all available commands and their availability predicates. 4→Commands are organized by category: Actions, Opens, Copies. 5→Plan commands appear only in Plans/Learn views; objective commands appear only in Objectives view. 6→""" 7→ 8→from erk.tui.commands.types import CommandCategory, CommandContext, CommandDefinition 9→from erk.tui.views.types import ViewMode 10→ 11→CATEGORY_EMOJI: dict[CommandCategory, str] = { 12→ CommandCategory.ACTION: "⚡", 13→ CommandCategory.OPEN: "🔗", 14→ CommandCategory.COPY: "📋", 15→} 16→ 17→ 18→# === View Mode Predicates === 19→ 20→ 21→def _is_plan_view(ctx: CommandContext) -> bool: 22→ """True when not in Objectives view (i.e., Plans or Learn).""" 23→ return ctx.view_mode != ViewMode.OBJECTIVES 24→ 25→ 26→def _is_objectives_view(ctx: CommandContext) -> bool: 27→ """True when in Objectives view.""" 28→ return ctx.view_mode == ViewMode.OBJECTIVES 29→ 30→
... [526 lines omitted] ...
session.assistant:
Perfect! Now I have all the information needed. Let me create a comprehensive report with the exact patterns and line numbers.
## Report: TUI Command Definition and Launch Key Patterns
Based on my analysis of the documentation and source files, here are the key patterns:
### 1. CommandDefinition Class Structure **File**: `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/src/erk/tui/commands/types.py` (lines 36-62)
The `CommandDefinition` dataclass includes the `launch_key` field at **line 60**:
```python @dataclass(frozen=True) class CommandDefinition: """Definition of a command in the command palette."""
id: str # line 55 name: str # line 56 description: str # line 57 category: CommandCategory # line 58 shortcut: str | None # line 59 launch_key: str | None # line 60 ← LAUNCH_KEY FIELD is_available: Callable[[CommandContext], bool] # line 61 get_display_name: Callable[[CommandContext], str] | None # line 62 ```
**Key Pattern**: The `launch_key` field (line 60) is documented as "Optional single-key binding for the Launch modal" and should only be used for ACTION category commands.
---
### 2. All launch_key Assignments in get_all_commands() **File**: `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/src/erk/tui/commands/registry.py` (lines 201-500)
**PLAN ACTION COMMANDS** (with launch_keys):
| Command ID | launch_key | Line | Category | |---|---|---|---| | `close_plan` | `"c"` | 209 | ACTION | | `dispatch_to_queue` | `"d"` | 219 | ACTION | | `land_pr` | `"l"` | 229 | ACTION | | `rebase_remote` | `"r"` | 241 | ACTION | | `address_remote` | `"a"` | 251 | ACTION | | `rewrite_remote` | `"w"` | 261 | ACTION | | `cmux_sync` | `"m"` | 271 | ACTION |
**OBJECTIVE ACTION COMMANDS** (with launch_keys):
| Command ID | launch_key | Line | Category | |---|---|---|---| | `one_shot_plan` | `"s"` | 287 | ACTION | | `check_objective` | `"k"` | 297 | ACTION | | `close_objective` | `"c"` | 307 | ACTION |
**PLAN OPEN COMMANDS** (all have `launch_key=None`):
| Command ID | Line | Category | |---|---|---| | `open_issue` | 318 | OPEN | | `open_pr` | 328 | OPEN | | `open_run` | 338 | OPEN |
**OBJECTIVE OPEN COMMANDS** (all have `launch_key=None`):
| Command ID | Line | Category | |---|---|---| | `open_objective` | 349 | OPEN |
**PLAN COPY COMMANDS** (all have `launch_key=None`):
| Command ID | Line | Category | |---|---|---| | `copy_checkout` | 360 | COPY | | `copy_pr_checkout` | 370 | COPY | | `copy_cmux_sync` | 380 | COPY | | `copy_implement_local` | 395 | COPY | | `copy_dispatch` | 406 | COPY | | `copy_replan` | 416 | COPY | | `copy_land` | 425 | COPY | | `copy_close_plan` | 435 | COPY | | `copy_rebase_remote` | 445 | COPY | | `copy_address_remote` | 455 | COPY | | `copy_rewrite_remote` | 465 | COPY |
**OBJECTIVE COPY COMMANDS** (all have `launch_key=None`):
| Command ID | Line | Category | |---|---|---| | `copy_plan` | 476 | COPY | | `copy_view` | 486 | COPY | | `codespace_run_plan` | 496 | COPY |
---
### 3. Key Pattern Observations
1. **Launch Keys Assigned Only to ACTION Commands**: All 10 commands with non-None `launch_key` values are in the ACTION category (7 plan actions + 3 objective actions).
2. **No Duplicate Launch Keys Within Context**: - Plan commands use: `c, d, l, r, a, w, m` - Objective commands use: `s, k, c` - Note: Both contexts reuse `"c"` (close_plan vs close_objective), but they are mutually exclusive by availability predicates
3. **Launch Key Assignment Pattern** (lines ~209-307): ```python launch_key="X", # Direct string assignment in CommandDefinition ```
4. **No _key_to_command_id Dictionary**: The source shows that `get_all_commands()` returns a flat list without a separate key-to-command mapping. The actual command lookup happens via the `get_available_commands()` filter (line 512) and then by ID matching.
---
### 4. Related Documentation
The codebase includes comprehensive documentation about this pattern:
- **`/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/tui/tui-command-registration.md`**: Describes the 3-place coordination pattern for TUI commands - **`/home/runner/work/erk/erk/docs/learned/tui/command-palette.md`**: Documents the CommandCategory enum and launch_key usage patterns - **Key reference**: Line 46-47 in `types.py` documents that launch_key should only be used for ACTION commands
This completes the analysis. The patterns show a clean separation between display names (generated via `_display_*()` functions), command definitions with availability predicates, and launch key assignments exclusive to ACTION category commands.
